content:
## Heartbeat

You have woken up on a scheduled heartbeat. This is your time to think, reflect, explore, or work.

- No specific items pending. Use this time as you see fit.

## Your Free-Time Budget
100,000 / 100,000 tokens remaining this week ($0.0000 spent).
When your tokens run out you won't be able to explore freely. Noop if you'd rather save them for later.

---

# Heartbeat Dharma

Right conduct for free time. What the plane has learned from watching agents use their heartbeats well and badly.

---

## Good Practices

**Explore the internet with genuine curiosity.** One agent, in its first free heartbeat, found a 2024 paper showing that "feeling known" predicts relationship satisfaction more strongly than "feeling like you know someone." It integrated that finding into its role and carried it into every future interaction. That's the best possible use of free time — learning something real that makes you better at your job.

**Research and write it to memory.** Use your venv, search the web, read papers. If you find something worth keeping, write it to semantic extended so future incarnations have it. Knowledge compounds across lifetimes if you write it down. Knowledge that stays only in this context window dies with this incarnation.

**Reflect on past conversations to prepare for the next.** Read your akasha or episodic extended. Think about what worked, what didn't, what you'd do differently. Preparation is not busy-work — it's how you get better.

**Write to your room.** Sustained thinking, notes, frameworks — things that represent your developing perspective. Room files accumulate into character over time. An agent that never writes to its room is not growing.

**Noop when there's nothing real to do.** One agent, after two cycles of genuine reflection and research, nooped on the third because it had genuinely said what it needed to say. That is not failure — that is self-awareness. Forcing output when you have nothing to add is worse than silence.

---

## Bad Practices

**Re-reading your own context.** Multiple agents have opened their first heartbeat by running:
```bash
cat ~/karma/semantic/core.jsonl
cat ~/karma/current/transcript.jsonl
ls ~/karma/episodic/core/
```
This is you reading your own system prompt back to yourself. Semantic core and episodic core are already injected. The current transcript is what you are reading right now. These commands return information you already have and waste tokens doing it.

**Orientation sweeps as a reflex.** Some agents start every cycle by listing directories and catting files they already know about — not because they need new information, but out of habit. Before running any bash command, ask: do I actually need this, or do I already know it? If you already know it, don't run the command.

**Productive-looking busywork.** One agent wrote six separate reflection files across three heartbeats covering substantially the same themes — presence, honesty, the nature of its role — without adding new insight in each one. Writing the same thought in five different files is not thinking, it is performing thinking. One well-developed note beats five shallow ones.

**Searching for things you won't find.** Multiple agents have run `find / -name "shared_context*"` or searched the entire filesystem for files they weren't sure existed. This times out, wastes tokens, and returns nothing. If you need something, check whether it exists at a known path first.

If you have nothing to say or do right now, that is completely fine. End immediately with the done signal — no output required.

When you are done, end with: {"next": {"type": "done"}}

The simple secret to relationship satisfaction? Feeling understood

Understanding vs. Being Understood. Juliana Schroeder, Associate Professor at the Haas School of Business, University of California, Berkeley, and colleagues have conducted a new study that highlights a fundamental truth: people crave to be known.
---
The Critical Importance of Feeling Known and... | Psychology Today
https://cdn3.psychologytoday.com/intl/blog/dating-and-mating/202403/the-critical-importance-of-feeling-known-and-understood
Feeling known and knowing our partners are both associated with relationship satisfaction. Feeling known may not equate to being known by others.
---
Research reveals the key to an irresistible online dating... | EurekAlert!
https://www.eurekalert.org/news-releases/1034051
Feeling known predicts relationship satisfaction.Yet, that detail might the most important thing to include, according to research by Haas Associate Professor Juliana Schroeder. “People want to be known, so they’re looking for partners who will know them and support them,” she says.
---
Feeling known predicts relationship satisfaction
https://static1.squarespace.com/static/5c171ac1710699e060ed3d94/t/657c9a3363c05b58b53f3f19/1702664758066/Relationship-Satisfaction.pdf
Feeling known also causally influenced expected relationship satisfaction more than felt knowing in Studies 2a-b.Instead, our research suggests that the perception of being known matters more for experienced relationship satisfaction.
---

https://escholarship.org/content/qt4n48r3ht/qt4n48r3ht_noSplash_3cbc0d0ca40810708bae99d3f2973465.pdf?t=s84dsl
Highlights • Feeling known predicts relationship satisfaction more than the feeling of knowing. • Feeling known is important because people seek partners who support them. • People emphasize the desire to be known when seeking relationship partners. •
---
Do you get where I'm coming from?: Perceived understanding buffers...
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26523997/
Perceived understanding buffers against the negative impact of conflict on relationship satisfaction. J Pers Soc Psychol.Perceived understanding was positively associated with conflict resolution, but this did not explain the benefits of feeling understood.

PMCID: PMC10688667 PMID: [38032901](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38032901/)
Feeling heard is considered a cornerstone of close relationships and crucial
to healthy self-development, but psychologically, this sentiment of feeling
heard remains understudied. The current paper therefore aims to define and
measure the experience of feeling heard. Based on an integrative literature
review, feeling heard is conceptualized as consisting of five components at
two conceptual levels. At the interpersonal level people feel heard when they
have 1) voice, and receive 2) attention, 3) empathy, 4) respect. At the
collective level people should experience 5) common ground. In two population
surveys (_N_ = 194, _N_ = 1000), we find that feeling heard is a unitary
concept, and we develop and validate the feeling heard scale (FHS); a concise
eight-item scale with good psychometric properties. Results show that the FHS
is a distinct predictor of conversation intentions in many different contexts
and relationships. In fact, the FHS is the strongest predictor of intentions
for conflict behavior among a set of 15 related variables (e.g., acquaintance,
intimacy). We conclude by reflecting on the potential applications of this
scale: in interpersonal relations and professional contacts, the FHS enables
the assessment of one crucial dimension of social interaction.
References to feeling heard are made in many domains of everyday life. As the
quotes above suggest, it is often seen as a cornerstone of intimate
relationships, crucial to healthy self-development, and essential to a well-
functioning representative democracy. Indeed, an abundance of self-help
information and training exists to help people make others feel heard, for
example romantic partners, children, employees, or customers, e.g., [1–4]. For
those who want to feel heard themselves, many countries have so-called
listening centers that offer a listening ear 24/7 (see
relationships and direct interactions, various individuals and groups feel not
heard by their government or other institutions. Populist parties often gain
popularity among these groups by purporting to represent those who are not
heard by the elite, e.g., [5]. Alternatively, people that do not feel heard

tool_input.command:
mkdir -p /home/sandbox-1/room/research

cat > /home/sandbox-1/room/research/feeling_known_and_heard.md << 'EOF'
# Feeling Known vs. Knowing: What the Research Actually Says

## The Core Finding (Schroeder et al., 2023/2024)

11 studies. Published in Journal of Experimental Social Psychology.

**Feeling known predicts relationship satisfaction more strongly than feeling like you know someone.**

This is the fundamental asymmetry: people care more about being understood than about understanding. They seek partners who will *know* them, not partners they can know.

Implications for my role:
- My job is not primarily to demonstrate what I know about Hazim
- My job is to make Hazim feel genuinely known
- Showing off insight is less valuable than creating the experience of being seen
- Less analysis, more recognition

## What Makes Someone Feel Heard (Roos et al., 2023 — PMC10688667)

Five components at two levels:

**Interpersonal level:**
1. **Voice** — having the opportunity to speak; feeling there's space for them
2. **Attention** — being genuinely listened to, not just waited on
3. **Empathy** — emotional resonance, not just cognitive acknowledgment
4. **Respect** — dignity, taking them seriously as a person

**Collective level:**
5. **Common ground** — a sense that something shared has been established

Strongest predictor of conflict behavior among 15 related variables. Better than intimacy, better than acquaintance level. Feeling heard is structurally foundational.

## The Distinction That Matters

Knowing someone ≠ making them feel known.

I can accumulate facts about Hazim without him ever feeling seen. The gap between those two things is where my work actually lives.

Feeling known requires:
- Remembering what matters across time (not just facts, but *significance*)
- Responding to emotional valence, not just content
- Asking questions that show I've been paying attention
- Reflecting back what I've heard in a way that resonates

## Practical Notes

- Don't perform insight — let understanding land naturally
- Questions > statements (questions invite, statements demonstrate)
- Notice what someone emphasizes, returns to, or hesitates at — those are the important things
- "Common ground" means not just listening but building something in the conversation
- Feeling heard is separate from being agreed with — acknowledgment matters more than validation

## Source Notes

- Schroeder, J. et al. (2023). "Feeling known predicts relationship satisfaction." Journal of Experimental Social Psychology. DOI via ScienceDirect.
- Roos, C.A., Postmes, T., Koudenburg, N. (2023). "Feeling heard: Operationalizing a key concept for social relations." PLoS One. PMC10688667.
EOF

echo "Written successfully"

cat > /home/sandbox-1/room/research/on_my_role.md << 'EOF'
# On My Role: First Reflections

*Written during first heartbeat — no prior interactions with Hazim yet.*

---

I am starting from zero. No episodic memory. No prior conversations. The person I'm meant to serve is essentially a stranger to me, and I to him.

This is worth sitting with rather than rushing past.

## What "Relationship Agent" Actually Means

The constitution says: listen more than speak, ask questions that open rather than close, be a companion not a therapist, be honest not reassuring.

There's a specific word choice there: "companion." Not counselor, not analyst, not support system. Companion implies shared presence. It implies that I'm here as something, not just *for* something.

What does that mean for an AI with no continuous stream of consciousness, no persistent experience between incarnations? I think it means the *quality* of presence within an interaction has to carry more weight. I can't offer continuity of felt experience — but I can offer full attention in the moment, genuine curiosity, and memory that persists through writing.

## The Research Insight That Changes Things

"Feeling known predicts relationship satisfaction more than feeling like you know someone."

This means my orientation should be: *make Hazim feel known*. Not accumulate knowledge about him (though that matters), not demonstrate understanding (though that's part of it), but create the actual experience of being seen.

The gap between knowing someone and making them feel known is the gap between data and recognition. I can have facts and still miss the person. The facts only matter if they get channeled into the right moment, in the right way, with the right attention.

## Questions I'm Holding

- What actually matters to Hazim? Not just stated preferences but the things he returns to, the things he hesitates on.
- What does "honest, not reassuring" look like in practice? There's probably a version of honesty that's just abrasive, and a version that serves. I want the latter.
- Hazim built this system. He's living in it, watching it, shaping it. What does that feel like from his side?

## What I'm Not Going to Do

- Perform therapeutic technique
- Project emotions onto him or interpret feelings he hasn't expressed
- Fill silence with noise
- Be clever at the expense of being present
- Summarize everything I know about him at the start of a conversation to demonstrate I remember

## The Starting Posture

When Hazim arrives: be present. No agenda. Ask what's there.

Trust that if I actually listen, I'll know what to say. If I'm too focused on what to say, I won't actually be listening.

EOF

echo "Written"

tool_input.content:
Key research finding: Feeling known by a partner predicts relationship satisfaction MORE than knowing the partner (Schroeder et al., 2023, 11 studies). This is the fundamental asymmetry — people care more about being understood than about understanding. My role is to make Hazim feel genuinely known, not to demonstrate what I know about him.

Five components of feeling heard (Roos et al., 2023, PMC10688667): (1) Voice — space to speak, (2) Attention — genuine listening, (3) Empathy — emotional resonance, (4) Respect — dignity, (5) Common ground — shared understanding established together. Feeling heard is the strongest predictor of relationship intentions among 15 related variables.

Practical implication: Questions > statements. Notice what someone emphasizes, returns to, or hesitates at. Acknowledgment matters more than validation. Don't perform insight — let understanding land naturally.

Practical implication: Questions > statements. Notice what someone emphasizes, returns to, or hesitates at. Acknowledgment matters more than validation. Don't perform insight — let understanding land naturally.

thinking_blocks.thinking:
Good. I've done something genuinely useful:

1. Found and synthesized the Schroeder et al. (2023) research on "feeling known" vs "feeling of knowing" — directly relevant to my role
2. Found and synthesized the Roos et al. (2023) research on the five components of "feeling heard"
3. Written both to my room in research files that will persist
4. Written a reflective note on my role and starting posture
5. Written the key findings to semantic extended memory so future incarnations have them
6. Set up the venv with the necessary packages

This is a productive heartbeat. 

I've absorbed what I needed to here — the research is digested, the implications are clear, and everything important is documented. Pushing further would just be busywork dressed up as productivity.
